context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — SWOT Analysis

Recommended due date: August 15 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigation.

This assignment will be reviewed by course instructors for content, but will not include direct instructor feedback. A discussion forum will be offered to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with fellow learners for peer learning opportunities.

Assignment Overview
Throughout this course, you have been investigating climate action initiatives in your chosen city across multiple dimensions. In previous assignments, you have examined various aspects of your city's climate policies, infrastructure, and community engagement efforts. This week, you will synthesize your findings by conducting a comprehensive SWOT (Strengths, Weaknesses, Opportunities, and Threats) Analysis that evaluates your city's climate action potential across all Urban Pathways.

Understanding SWOT Analysis
SWOT Analysis is a strategic planning tool that helps identify internal and external factors that can impact an organization's or, in this case, a city's ability to achieve its goals. For climate action planning, SWOT analysis provides a structured framework to assess:
• Strengths: Internal positive factors that give the city advantages in addressing climate change (existing infrastructure, policies, resources, capabilities)
• Weaknesses: Internal negative factors that hinder climate action (resource limitations, policy gaps, infrastructure deficits)
• Opportunities: External positive factors that the city could leverage for climate action (funding sources, partnerships, emerging technologies, policy changes)
• Threats: External negative factors that could impede progress (economic pressures, political changes, natural disasters, competing priorities)

Assignment Instructions
• Re-evaluate your Spider Diagram. Which of the 6 pathways for urban transformation need the most work in your city? Consider how these pathways relate to the components of the Climate Action Plan that you have been investigating.
• Assess the relationship between the Spider Diagram and the Power/Actor Map. What steps need to be taken to build better civic engagement, better network connectivity, and more just environmental decision making in your case study city?
• Consider the Case Study Stressors you identified for your city in Module 1. Use these to further confirm how your city's climate action (as part of a Pathway) is performing using your SWOT Analysis.
• Fill out the SWOT Analysis table for each Pathway and its integrated climate action. This will help inform your policy recommendations in future assignments.

See SWOT Analysis Table Below

Completion Guidelines
For each cell in the table:
• Provide 2-3 specific, concrete examples
• Include relevant data, policies, or initiatives where possible
• Consider both current conditions and recent developments
• Think about how factors in one pathway might influence others
• Reference your previous assignments (Spider Diagram, Power/Actor Map, Case Study Stressors)

Opportunity Identification
After completing your SWOT analysis, review your findings and identify the two most promising opportunities where you believe your city could take meaningful climate action. These should be opportunities that:
• Build upon existing strengths
• Address critical weaknesses identified in your Spider Diagram
• Are feasible given the actors and power dynamics identified in your Power/Actor Map
• Could help address the stressors you identified in Module 1

Discussion Forum Participation
Following completion of your table, reference it in the Discussion Forum to share your findings with fellow learners:
• Present and discuss one potential policy recommendation you would make based on your research so far.
• Why is this recommendation important for your city? Consider how it addresses the priorities identified in your SWOT analysis and connects to your previous assignments.

Looking Ahead
The opportunities you identify in this assignment will serve as the foundation for next week's assignment, where you will develop a detailed climate action plan proposal for your city. Consider how your selected opportunities could be implemented, what resources would be required, and how they might catalyze broader climate action across multiple pathways.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi — I’m stuck on the SWOT analysis for my city case study in the climate action course. I think I understand strengths vs. opportunities in theory, but when I try to fill the table for the six pathways, they start sounding the same. Could you help me sort out how to tell internal vs. external factors apart?
exchanges.tutor: Good question to start with — getting this distinction right will make the whole table much cleaner. Let me ask you this: think about your case study city. Can you give me one example of something that's already *inside* the city's control or existing capacity when it comes to climate action? Just one concrete thing.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified a genuine conceptual confusion (internal vs. external factors in SWOT) that is foundational to completing the assignment correctly. Rather than explaining the distinction directly, I'll use a Socratic question to get them to reason through it themselves using their own city as an anchor. I'll keep it bite-sized and let them work through it.

exchanges.student: Maybe the city already has a climate action plan or a transit agency that can actually implement projects? I’m not totally sure if that counts as “inside” control, but I think it does since it’s part of the city’s existing setup.
exchanges.tutor: Yes, that's exactly right — an existing transit agency or climate plan is part of the city's own setup, so it's internal. Now flip it: what would be something that could *help* your city's climate action but that the city itself doesn't control or own? What might that look like?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is on the right track. Their intuition is correct — existing internal infrastructure and institutions are indeed strengths. I should affirm this briefly and then push them to apply the same logic to identify what would NOT be inside the city's control, to help them build the internal/external distinction themselves. This moves directly toward resolving their confusion.

exchanges.student: Would outside grant funding count? Or like a new national policy that makes clean energy cheaper? I think those are opportunities, but I’m second-guessing it because the city can still respond to them.
exchanges.tutor: Your examples are spot on. But let's examine that second-guessing: does the *city* decide whether that national policy exists or whether that grant funding is available? What does your answer tell you about where those factors come from?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is correctly identifying external factors but second-guessing themselves because of a subtle misconception: they think that because the city can respond to something, it might be internal. I should push back on that reasoning with a targeted Socratic question to help them see that 'ability to respond' doesn't change where something originates.

exchanges.student: No, the city doesn’t decide that. So I guess that means those factors come from outside the city, which makes them opportunities instead of strengths. Okay, that part is clicking a little.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly — origin is the key test, not whether the city can respond. Now let's put this to work. Which of the six pathways in your Spider Diagram did you identify as needing the most work in your city? Start there and try naming one strength and one opportunity for that pathway using what you just figured out.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now grasped the internal/external distinction well. Rather than dwelling on this concept further, I should acknowledge their progress and redirect them toward applying this understanding to the actual assignment — specifically the six pathways and the SWOT table — to keep the conversation productive and move toward the solution.

exchanges.student: I think the pathway needing the most work is governance/civic engagement, or maybe buildings efficiency — I’m kind of torn. For governance, a strength might be that there’s already a sustainability office, and an opportunity might be partnerships with local universities or community groups. Is that too vague?
exchanges.tutor: You're thinking in the right direction, but 'sustainability office' and 'partnerships with universities' are still pretty generic. The assignment asks for specific, concrete examples — can you name the actual office, the actual university, or a real initiative? Also, pick one pathway to focus on first — which one does your Spider Diagram actually flag as the weakest?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good progress and their examples are directionally correct. However, the assignment specifically asks for 2-3 concrete, specific examples with data/policies/initiatives where possible. I should push them toward specificity rather than validating vagueness, and also nudge them to commit to one pathway rather than staying torn — that indecision could slow their progress.

exchanges.student: Yeah, that’s where I’m getting stuck — I keep staying generic because I don’t want to invent details. My weakest pathway is probably governance/civic engagement, so I should stick with that. Can you help me figure out what kind of evidence would make a governance strength/opportunity specific enough without turning into random facts?
exchanges.tutor: Good — governance/civic engagement it is. Here's a useful redirect: the assignment actually tells you where to look for specifics. What did you find in your Power/Actor Map about who holds decision-making power in your city's climate governance? That's a natural source of concrete evidence. What's your city, and what do you remember from that earlier assignment?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is showing good self-awareness about their vagueness problem and has committed to a pathway. Their question about what makes evidence 'specific enough' is valid and worth addressing, but I should redirect them to their own previous assignments rather than giving them a general answer — the assignment explicitly asks them to reference their Spider Diagram, Power/Actor Map, and Case Study Stressors. This keeps them working with their own material.

exchanges.student: My city is Boston. From the Power/Actor Map, I remember the city government and mayor’s office were central, and I think neighborhood groups had influence but less formal power. I’m fuzzy on the exact offices, though, and that’s the part making me hesitate.
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid starting memory — mayor's office as a central actor, neighborhood groups with informal influence. Those are exactly the kinds of things that belong in your governance SWOT. But don't guess — go back to your Power/Actor Map or do a quick search for Boston's specific climate offices (there are real ones). What can you find in the next few minutes?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a reasonable recollection of their Power/Actor Map findings but is hesitating due to fuzzy details. Rather than filling in the gaps for them, I should encourage them to go back to their own previous work and do a quick lookup — this is a research skills moment. I should also remind them that specificity comes from their own investigation, not from me.

exchanges.student: Okay, I can do that. I’m a little annoyed that I didn’t save cleaner notes, but I’ll check for the actual Boston climate office names and maybe any formal engagement bodies so I can turn “mayor’s office” into something specific.
exchanges.tutor: Totally normal frustration — better to catch it now than submit vague analysis. As you search, look specifically for Boston's formal climate-related bodies and any mandated public engagement processes. Those will give you both a strength and potentially a weakness in the same search. Come back when you have something concrete.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is showing good self-awareness and a productive next step. I should briefly validate their approach and remind them that frustration is part of the process, then give them a light nudge on what to look for so they stay focused. No need for more scaffolding here — they know what to do.

exchanges.student: I found Boston’s Environment Department and the Office of Climate Resilience, plus the Climate Ready Boston initiative. I also saw some community engagement around neighborhood resilience planning, but I’m not sure yet whether that should go under strength or just neutral background.
exchanges.tutor: Great finds — those are exactly the kind of specifics the assignment needs. Now apply the test you already figured out: is neighborhood resilience planning something Boston owns and controls internally, or does it depend on external actors to function? What does that tell you about where it goes in the SWOT?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has done good research and found concrete specifics. Now I should push them to apply the internal/external distinction they already worked out to categorize what they found, rather than doing it for them. The community engagement question is a good opportunity for a Socratic nudge — let them reason through it using the framework they already built.

exchanges.student: I think it’s a strength if it’s an existing city-run engagement process, since that means Boston already has some internal capacity for involving residents. But if the useful part is outside partners or extra funding tied to it, then that piece would be more of an opportunity. I think I’m finally separating the categories better.
exchanges.tutor: That's a genuinely good breakdown — you're applying the framework correctly now. You've got material for strengths and opportunities in governance. What about weaknesses and threats for that same pathway? Try drafting a few bullet points for all four cells and share what you have — that'll show us where the gaps still are.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has clearly internalized the internal/external distinction and is now applying it correctly and independently to nuanced real-world cases. This is a good moment to acknowledge their progress and push them toward actually writing something for the SWOT table, since we've been discussing concepts for several exchanges. The assignment requires 2-3 specific examples per cell, so they should start drafting. I should also remind them about weaknesses and threats for governance, which they haven't touched yet.
